content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Bill Kampe, Mayor 
City of Pacific Grove 
300 Forest Ave 
Pacific Grove, CA 93950 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-16-267 
Dear Mayor Kampe: 
January 30, 2017 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of 
the Political Refonn Act (the "Act").1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the 
Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of 
interest or Section 1090. Moreover, this letter is based on the facts presented. The Fair Political 
Practices Commission does not act as a finder of fact when it renders advice. (In re Oglesby ( 1975) 
1 FPPC Ops. 71.) 
QUESTION 
Under the Act, may you participate in decisions related to the Local Coastal Program, which 
includes the Land Use Plan and Implementation Plan, where your residential real property is within 
500 feet of the affected Coastal Zone? 
CONCLUSION 
You may participate in the Local Coastal Program decisions so long as the decisions do not 
present a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your residential real property. In 
the event that you have a conflict of interest, you may be able to participate in decisions that can be 
segmented or under the "public generally exception.'' Please review the discussion below. 
FACTS 
Background on Local Coastal Programs:2 The Coastal Act requires each coastal city and 
county to prepare a Local Coastal Program ("LCP") that establishes the kind, location, and intensity 
1 The Political Refonn Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 ofTitle 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-16-267 
Page No. 2 
of land and water uses appropriate to its portion of the Coastal Zone, as well as the resource 
protection standards for any future development. Each unique LCP must conform to the California 
Coastal Act's goals and policies and be approved by the Coastal Commission. The LCP is 
comprised of a "Land Use Plan" and an "Implementation Plan." 
Currently, the Coastal Commission has jurisdiction over the City of Pacific Grove's Coastal 
Zone because the City does not have a finalized or certified Implementation Plan, and thereby lacks 
a completed LCP. 3 Pacific Grove's proposed LCP governs land use, development, parks and open 
space in the Pacific Grove Coastal Zone. Upon approval, the City's LCP will be the legally binding 
standard ofreview for issuing Coastal Development Permits for activities within most of the Pacific 
Grove Coastal Zone. It also becomes the coastal element of the Pacific Grove General Plan. 
You state that the City's proposed LCP will be considered in two phases. First the Council 
will be briefed on the structure of the Land Use Plan, its relationship to the General Plan, and the 
role of implementing ordinances. In the second phase, the Council will review and adopt specific 
elements and text proposed in the Implementation Plan. 
The Land Use Plan provides policy direction regarding coastal land use and development. It 
also includes a land use map that shows generally the uses that are appropriate in each area, maps of 
sensitive biological resources, and maps of other coastal resources, such as coastal public 
accessways and scenic resources. 
The Implementation Plan includes relevant portions of the zoning code applicable to the 
Coastal Zone and other programs needed to carry out the goals, policies, and land use designations 
of the Land Use Plan. The document lists allowable land uses for each zoning district, implements 
appropriate height, mass, and setback requirements for development, and specifies the coastal 
resources protection standards that allowable development must meet, all of which must be based 
upon Land Use Plan policies. The Implementation Plan contains zoning maps that show which 
zoning rules apply to each lot. In addition, it contains procedural requirements that govern the types 
of projects requiring a Coastal Development Permit, how a Coastal Development Permit can be 
obtained, and the opportunities for public participation in Coastal Development Permit review. 4 
Pacific Grove's Coastal Zone is comprised of approximately 458 acres ofland. 
Development within that zone, for the most part, requires issuance of a Coastal Development 
Permit. You provided a map with your request prepared by the City Planning Department. The map 
states the size of the City as 1,842 acres of land, 25% of which is located in the Coastal Zone, and 
44% of which is in and within 500 feet of the Coastal Zone. 5 Looking at population, 11 % of the 
City's population is in the Coastal Zone and 25% are in and within 500 feet of the Coastal Zone. 
You also state that three of the seven City Council members are also within 500 feet of the Coastal 
Zone. 
1 The Coastal Commission certified a predecessor Land Use Plan in 1989, but the City never finalized or 
received certification of the Implementation Plan. (City of Pacific Grove, Final Land Use Plan, supra, page I.) 
4 Id., page 7. 
s You additionally noted that Pacific Grove is unique in having jurisdiction for a marine protected area and that 
the total acreage for the City including water and land is 2,561 acres. 


File No. A-16-267 
Page No. 3 
You are mayor of the City of Pacific Grove. Your residential real property is within 500 feet 
of the Coastal Zone subject to the LCP. You state that the current proposed changes to zoning of 
Coastal Zone properties would affect properties about a mile distant from your residence. 
ANALYSIS 
Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using 
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial interest. 
A conflict of interest may arise only when it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a 
material financial effect on the official or his or her immediate family that is distinguishable from 
its effect on the public generally. (Section 87103.) 
At issue here is your financial interest in your residential real property located within 500 
feet of the Coastal Zone subject to the LCP decisions. 
Reaso11ably Foreseeable: For a financial interest that is not explicitly involved in a decision 
(such as your property), Regulation 18701(b) states that a financial effect can be reasonably 
foreseeable where it is a realistic possibility under ordinary circumstances. 
Matedal Financial Effect: Regulation 18702.2( a) sets the materiality standard for real 
property interests and provides a list of circumstances to consider. As relevant to your facts, the 
financial effect will be material if the decisions: 
(10) Would change the character of the parcel of real property by substantially 
altering traffic levels or intensity of use, including parking, of property 
surrounding the official's real property parcel, the view, privacy, noise levels, or 
air quality, including odors, or any other factors that would affect the market value 
of the real property parcel in which the official has a financial interest; 
( 11) Would consider any decision affecting real property value located within 500 
feet of the property line of the official's real property . . .. Notwithstanding this 
prohibition, the Commission may provide written advice allowing an official to 
participate under these circumstances if the Commission detennines that there are 
sufficient facts to indicate that there will be no reasonably foreseeable measurable 
impact on the official's property; or 
(12) Would cause a reasonably prudent person, using due care and consideration 
under the circumstances, to believe that the governmental decision was of such a 
nature that its reasonably foreseeable effect would influence the market value of 
the official's property. 
(Regulation l 8702.2(a).) 
Conflicts must be analyzed on a decision-by-decision basis. You have not identified any 
specific LCP Land Use Plan or lmplementation Plan decisions at issue, but make a general inquiry 
as to the types of decisions that may be involved, including those that affect property over 500 feet 


File No. A-16-267 
Page No. 4 
from your property. Two years ago we addressed a similar general request regarding LCP decisions 
for a Pacific Grove Council member in the Peake Advice Letter No. A-15-227.6 As in the Peake 
Letter, we address three possible decision scenarios, most relevant to your situation, and advise that 
you contact us for additional formal advice if a particular decision implicates the materiality factors 
noted above. 
1. Decisions affecting specific coastal areas further than 500 feet from your property. 
You noted that the proposed zoning changes to Coastal Zone properties would affect 
properties "about a mile distant from your residence." Generally, you will not have a conflict of 
interest in decisions that affect areas distant from your property unless the materiality factors in 
items ( I 0) and ( 12), listed above are implicated. For example, if the decision (such as with a large 
development project) altered traffic, privacy or parking in the area. You should seek advice if you 
believe a significant decision within the LCP may affect your property despite being beyond 500 
feet. 
2. Decisions uniform(v affecting the elltire Coastal Zone. 
Under the "public generally" exception, a public official does not have a prohibited conflict 
of interest under the Act, if the decision's effect on the official is the same as the effect on the 
general public. (Section 87103.) To qualify for this exception an official must establish that a 
"significant segment" of the public is affected, and that the official's financial effect is not unique 
compared to the significant segment. (Regulation 18703.) Regulation 18703 defines a "significant 
segment" as: 
(b) . .. at least 25 percent of: 
( 1) All businesses or non-profit entities within the official's jurisdiction; 
(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property within 
the official's jurisdiction; or 
(3} All individuals within the official's jurisdiction. 
Your residential real property is not in the Coastal Zone but will be impacted indirectly by 
its proximity. Therefore, the effects of uniform decisions will most likely be shared by other 
properties near the Coastal Zone. The map you provided states the size of the City as 1,842 acres 
(reflecting land) and that the 25% of the City real property is in the Coastal Zone and 44% of the 
total real property is in the Coastal Zone and within 500 feet. Since more than 25% of all the City's 
real property is in or within 500 feet of the Coastal Zone, you can establish that a ''significant 
segment" is affected by a decision. 
6 Please note that the Peake Letter provided the City's total acreage as 2,561 acres; with a Coastal Zone at 
17.9% of the City's total area; and all City areas within 500 feet of the Coastal Zone boundary at approximately 30% of 
the City's total area. 


File No. A-16-267 
Page No. 5 
Regulation 18703(c) defines a "unique effect" as a disproportionate effect on: 
(1) The development potential or use of the official's real property or on the 
income producing potential of the official's real property or business entity. 
(2) An official's business entity or real property resulting from the proximity of a 
project that is the subject of a decision. 
(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from the 
cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
(4) An official's interest in a business entity or real property resulting from the 
official's substantially greater business volume or larger real property size when a 
decision affects all interests by the same or similar rate or percentage. 
(5) A person's income, investments, assets or liabilities, or real property if the 
person is a source of income or gifts to the official. 
(6) An official's personal finances or those of his or her immediate family. 
If none of the unique effect criteria above apply to your circumstances, the public generally 
exception could apply and you may participate in the decision. 
3. Decisions 1111iq11ely affecting coastal areas near your real property. 
Lastly, you did not indicate that any of the LCP decisions will uniquely affect areas near 
your real property. If such a decision would affect real property value located within 500 feet of 
your real property, then you will most likely have a conflict of interest. (Regulation 
I 8702.2(a)(l l ).) You will be prohibited from participating in any such decisions. 
Please note that you may be able to participate in certain decisions which can be segmented 
from the decisions for which you have a conflict of interest. Regulation 18706 provides the method 
for segmentation of a governmental decision as follows: 
(a) An agency may segment a decision in which a public official has a financial 
interest, to allow participation by the official, provided all of the following 
conditions apply: 
( l) The decision in which the official has a financial interest can be broken down 
into separate decisions that are not inextricably interrelated to the decision in 
which the official has a disqualifying financial interest; 
(2) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is segmented from 
the other decisions; 


File No. A-16-267 
Page No. 6 
(3) The decision in which the official has a financial interest is considered first 
and a final decision is reached by the agency without the disqualified official's 
participation in any way; and 
(4) Once the decision in which the official has a financial interest has been made, 
the disqualified public official's participation does not result in a reopening of, 
or otherwise financially affect, the decision from which the official was 
disqualified. 
For purposes of this regulation, decisions are "inextricably interrelated" when the result of 
one decision will effectively detennine, affinn, nullify, or alter the result of another decision. 
(Regulation l 8706(b ).) 
Please contact our office for advice specific to a particular decision if it will implicate any of 
the noted materiality factors. 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Hyla P. Wagner 
General Counsel 
;I? r-- 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Under the Act, may you participate in decisions related to the Local Coastal Program, which 
includes the Land Use Plan and Implementation Plan, where your residential real property is within 
500 feet of the affected Coastal Zone?
sections.conclusion:
You may participate in the Local Coastal Program decisions so long as the decisions do not 
present a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your residential real property. In 
the event that you have a conflict of interest, you may be able to participate in decisions that can be 
segmented or under the "public generally exception.'' Please review the discussion below.

includes the Land Use Plan and Implementation Plan, where your residential real property is within 
500 feet of the affected Coastal Zone?

CONCLUSION: You may participate in the Local Coastal Program decisions so long as the decisions do not 
present a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect on your residential real property. In 
the event that you have a conflict of interest, you may be able to participate in decisions that can be 
segmented or under the "public generally exception.'' Please review the discussion below.
